eras.i: Empire by other names. The monetary fight Bryan lost twice to McKinley. A central bank. The 1929 crash. A New Deal that briefly held the bust in place. A postwar order built around a dollar pegged to gold.
eras.ii: After the war, an intelligence apparatus designed for a global contest. Where elected governments became inconvenient, it removed them. Iran. Guatemala. Indonesia. Chile. The body count from this period runs into the millions. By 1975 the public read enough of the agencies' own files to ask what else.
eras.iii: The dollar comes off gold. Reagan rolls back the postwar policy stack. September 2001 reopens the playbook for foreign wars. The bailouts that follow run the opposite direction of the New Deal's.
eras.iv: Citizens United legalizes the money. Snowden documents the mass collection. The Epstein files surface and disappear. Gaza is funded, armed, and shielded. Palantir, OpenAI, and the data-center race come for the rest.
eras.v: Eras I through IV document the systems. Era V documents the people running them — named, on the record, with primary sources and witnesses.

anchors.y1898: The Spanish-American War. By 1898 the United States had taken Cuba, Puerto Rico, Guam, the Philippines, and Hawaii. A continental republic acquired an overseas empire in a single year, and built the institutions to administer it.
anchors.y1900-mckinley: William McKinley defeats William Jennings Bryan for the second time. Bryan's “Cross of Gold” platform — bimetallism, free silver, looser money for the indebted agricultural classes — loses. McKinley's program — gold standard, high tariffs, overseas expansion — wins. Frank Baum publishes The Wizard of Oz the same year; scholars since Henry Littlefield's 1964 essay have read it as a monetary allegory of the election (yellow brick road = gold, silver slippers = silver, the wizard = the financial interests). Donald Trump has cited McKinley repeatedly since 2024 as a model — the tariffs, the territorial ambitions.
anchors.y1913: The Federal Reserve Act creates a central banking system run by a board of governors with regional reserve banks owned by member commercial banks. The architecture mixes public and private authority over the money supply. The mechanic — that commercial banks create the money when they lend, but do not create the interest — was documented in plain language by the Bank of England in its 2014 Quarterly Bulletin.
anchors.y1929-crash: From its September 3, 1929 peak of 381 to its July 8, 1932 trough of 41.22, the Dow falls 89%. The 1920s run-up was substantially leveraged on margin. Between August 1929 and March 1933, the U.S. money supply contracted by more than a third — what Milton Friedman and Anna Schwartz called the Great Contraction. Wages fell sharply. Debts, fixed by contract, did not.
anchors.y1933: Banking holiday, deposit insurance, Glass–Steagall, the SEC, public works at scale. Under the postwar policy stack — high top marginal tax rates, strong unions, regulated banking, Bretton Woods — measured U.S. inequality fell to its lowest level. Real median household income roughly doubled between 1947 and 1973.
anchors.y1944: Forty-four allied nations meet at a New Hampshire resort and design the postwar monetary order around a U.S. dollar pegged to gold. The IMF and the World Bank are created here. The arrangement holds until Nixon ends gold convertibility in 1971.
anchors.y1947: The National Security Act creates the CIA. Fifteen years later, the Joint Chiefs would formally propose remote-controlling civilian aircraft and “orchestrating terrorism in U.S. cities” as a pretext for invading Cuba. Kennedy rejected it. The declassified document is filed under this entry.
anchors.y1948-genocide-convention: The Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide is adopted at the United Nations. It protects national, ethnical, racial, and religious groups. On Soviet insistence with assent from other states, it does not protect political groups. Mass killings carried out for political reasons therefore fall outside the Convention's reach.
anchors.y1953: Operation Ajax. The CIA and MI6 coordinate the overthrow of Iran's democratically elected prime minister, Mohammad Mosaddegh, after his government nationalizes the Anglo-Iranian Oil Company. The CIA's own internal history, released in 2013, confirms its role.
anchors.y1954: Operation PBSUCCESS. The CIA backs the coup against Jacobo Árbenz of Guatemala — democratically elected, in office two years — under heavy lobbying from the United Fruit Company. The thirty-six-year civil war that follows kills more than 200,000 people. The U.N.-backed truth commission concludes that 83% of the dead were Mayan and 93% of human-rights violations were carried out by state forces.
anchors.y1965: October 1965 through March 1966. The Indonesian army and army-sponsored militias kill an estimated 500,000 to 1,000,000 people associated with the Indonesian Communist Party. The U.S. embassy in Jakarta passes lists of names to the Indonesian generals, knowing they will be used as kill lists. A 1968 CIA research study ranks the result among the worst mass murders of the twentieth century, alongside the Soviet purges and the Nazi exterminations. The CIA's own assessment, declassified, is filed under this entry.
anchors.y1965-vietnam: U.S. ground combat in Vietnam begins in March 1965. By the time the war ends in 1975, the Vietnamese government's 1995 estimate places civilian deaths at roughly two million and PAVN/Viet Cong dead at 1.1 million. The war also spills into Cambodia and Laos, neither of them U.S. enemies on paper; the Cambodian death toll runs to 275,000–310,000 and the Laotian to 20,000–62,000.
anchors.y1973: September 11, 1973. The democratically elected Allende government in Chile is overthrown in a coup whose covert U.S. financial and operational support was later documented by the Senate Church Committee.
anchors.y1975: The Church Committee — a U.S. Senate select committee chaired by Frank Church — spends a year examining the conduct of the CIA, FBI, NSA, and IRS. On the hearing-room table: the CIA's silent dart pistol, modified to fire a frozen shellfish-toxin pellet, alongside testimony on Chile, on Cuba, on the Phoenix Program. The Committee's reports remain the single largest declassification of intelligence-agency conduct in U.S. history.
anchors.y1975-condor: Operation Condor. Six South American military regimes — Argentina, Chile, Uruguay, Paraguay, Bolivia, Brazil — run a coordinated, cross-border program of assassination, disappearance, and torture against political opponents, on infrastructure and communications the United States helped supply. Estimates of those killed range from 60,000 to 80,000, with about 400,000 political prisoners taken. The U.S. cables that document the program were declassified in the 1990s and 2000s.
anchors.y1971: Nixon closes the gold window. The Bretton Woods arrangement ends; the dollar becomes a fiat currency.
anchors.y1980: Reagan's election ratifies a new economic compact: top marginal tax rates collapse, organized labor is rolled back, financial regulation is loosened, and the social-services baseline begins a thirty-year decline. From 1979 onward, U.S. productivity and the pay of the typical worker — which had moved together since the war — separate. The wealth share of the top 1% rises from roughly 22% in the late 1970s to roughly 34% by 2007. The administration's Central America policy is documented in the next entry.
anchors.y1980-el-salvador: Twelve years of civil war in El Salvador, 1980–1992, kill approximately 75,000 civilians. The U.N. Truth Commission's report finds that 85% of acts of violence against civilians were carried out by state forces, paramilitaries, and death squads — the same forces the U.S. trained, equipped, and funded. The Atlacatl Battalion, U.S.-trained, carried out the 1981 El Mozote massacre.
anchors.y1998-iraq-sanctions: Denis Halliday, the United Nations' Humanitarian Coordinator for Iraq, resigns after thirty-four years at the U.N. rather than continue administering the Security Council's economic sanctions on Iraq. He publicly calls them genocide. His successor, Hans von Sponeck, resigns for the same reason two years later. Halliday cites contemporary estimates of one to one and a half million Iraqi dead from malnutrition and inadequate health care under the sanctions regime.
anchors.y2001-attack: An event still being argued about. The records below cover the verifiable threads from on and around the day itself — questions about timing, physical evidence, and who knew what when. Silverstein's lease, signed weeks before. Zim's lease, ended weeks before. The Dancing Israelis. The gold in the WTC 4 vault. The records that burned in WTC 7. The passport in the rubble. Hanjour's piloting reports. The Odigo instant messages. The Jennings lobby account. None of these prove anything on their own. They are the questions, with citations.
anchors.y2001-aftermath: A CIA officer goes on television in 2007 to confirm the agency tortured. He is prosecuted in 2013. The Senate publishes its own study in 2014 — 6,700 pages, of which 525 are made public.
anchors.y2003: The invasion of Iraq, justified by claims about weapons of mass destruction that did not exist. The intelligence apparatus that produced the case is the same one whose conduct the Senate documented in 2014. Direct and indirect casualty estimates from the war run into the hundreds of thousands.
anchors.y2008: The global financial crisis. Banks that bought one another's mortgage-backed securities discover they are all insolvent in the same week. Public balance sheets absorb the loss; the executives who built the position keep their bonuses. A 2011 GAO audit — the first audit of its kind, mandated by the Dodd–Frank Act — documents approximately $16 trillion in cumulative gross Federal Reserve emergency lending across the crisis facilities. From 2009 to 2012, the top 1% captured 95% of all real-income growth in the recovery (Saez). Roughly eight million U.S. households lost their homes.
anchors.y2010: Citizens United v. FEC. The Supreme Court rules that political spending is speech and that corporations have the same speech rights as people.
anchors.y2013: Edward Snowden hands the Guardian and the Washington Post a complete archive of NSA bulk-collection programs — PRISM, Boundless Informant, Stellar Wind — and flees the country. The legal arguments the NSA had been making in secret are revealed to have been false in public. The infrastructure he documented is still in operation.
anchors.y2017-bennington: Four high-profile cultural figures — Chris Cornell (Soundgarden, May 2017), Chester Bennington (Linkin Park, July 2017), Avicii (Tim Bergling, April 2018), and Anthony Bourdain (CNN, June 2018) — die in fourteen months. Each death is individually ruled suicide. A claimed documentary tying them together — “The Silent Children” — does not appear in any verifiable production record, but the speculation it represents has circulated continuously since. What is documented is each death itself, and the FBI's own 2007 bulletin on the symbols pedophiles use to identify themselves.
anchors.y2019: Jeffrey Epstein is arrested July 6, 2019. He is found dead in his Manhattan jail cell on August 10. The two guards assigned to his floor sign false log entries. The two cameras pointed at his cell malfunction. By the next year, Ghislaine Maxwell has been arrested and convicted; many of his named clients have not. The opening poll on this page records what the public thinks happened.
anchors.y2020-pandemic-response: March 2020. The S&P 500 falls 34% in a month. The Federal Reserve expands its balance sheet by approximately $3 trillion in three months. The market recovers the prior peak within five months. U.S. billionaire wealth increases by approximately $1.3 trillion between March and September 2020. Unemployment peaks at 14.7%, falling hardest on hourly workers with no assets to cushion it. Real average hourly earnings then decline for 25 consecutive months as post-stimulus inflation outruns wages.
anchors.y2021-surfside: 1:22 a.m., June 24, 2021. The Champlain Towers South in Surfside, Florida partially collapses. 98 dead. NIST traces the cause to the pool deck, the corroded reinforcing steel, and warnings that had been on paper since 2018. Around the same hours, separately, John McAfee dies in a Spanish prison cell. The two threads are real and documented. What ties them together, if anything, is what the rest of the page records.
anchors.y2023: October 7, 2023 begins the latest phase of the conflict in Gaza. The Max Planck Institute for Demographic Research estimates more than 100,000 conflict-related deaths in Gaza by October 2025, with life expectancy falling to nearly half what it would have been without the war. The campaign is funded, armed, and diplomatically shielded by the United States.
anchors.y2024-pagers: September 17–18, 2024. Thousands of pagers and walkie-talkies in use by Hezbollah operatives explode at once across Lebanon and Syria. Israel acknowledges responsibility weeks later.
anchors.y2024: Palantir's defense contracts compound. Its Maven Smart System becomes the U.S. military's targeting backbone. Microsoft, Oracle, OpenAI, and Amazon Web Services line up on the same side of the same procurement pipeline.
anchors.y2025-kirk: Charlie Kirk is shot once in the neck at a university debate in Utah, in front of 3,000 people. The accused shooter is identified and charged within 48 hours. The official ballistic, DNA, and witness record is unambiguous. Alternative explanations have circulated — including the claim that an exploding lavalier microphone, not a bullet, was the source of the wound, and assertions that other people in the field of view were involved. Each of those claims is documented under this entry alongside what the official record actually shows.
anchors.y2025: Where the page lands as of mid-2026. The threads above are presented together, with citations, so the reader can assess them.
anchors.y2021-anthropic: December 2020. A group of safety researchers — including Dario Amodei (then VP of Research at OpenAI) and his sister Daniela (then VP of Safety & Policy) — leave to start Anthropic. They incorporate as a public-benefit corporation. The stated reason: a different vision for how AI should be scaled, and how the people building it should be trusted.
anchors.y2023-altman-firing: November 17, 2023. The OpenAI board fires Sam Altman, citing a lack of consistent candor in his communications. Five days later he is reinstated. Three of the four board members who voted to remove him are gone within weeks. The board's reasoning, and what was redacted from the public account of it, is the through-line that Ronan Farrow picks up two years later.
anchors.y2026-altman: April 2026. Ronan Farrow and Andrew Marantz publish 16,000 words in the New Yorker, the product of eighteen months of reporting, dozens of named-and-on-record sources, internal memos, and a sitting senior Microsoft executive who compares Altman to Madoff and SBF.
anchors.y2026-ai-bubble: U.S. AI capital expenditure is running at roughly 5% of GDP. The historical bar at which infrastructure buildouts have busted the economy is 3%. Railroads lasted a century. Highways, fifty years. Fiber, thirty. The GPUs in this round of capex obsolesce every three to four. Daniel Priestley, who called the 2008 crash early, told Steven Bartlett what he is now saying about 2029.
